issues: Opening 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' is a header addition not in the source
'want of the questioner' is slightly archaic; Greek ἐπιθυμίας = desire/longing, not 'want'
'It looks like a betrayal' — Greek κίνδυνος προδοσίας = 'danger of betrayal', not 'looks like'; subtle shift
'intellectual energies God gives you' — Greek ὁρμαῖς = impulses/urgings, not quite 'energies'
Prose is competent but somewhat stiff and 19th-century in register
strengths: Faithful rendering of the theological vocabulary (θεολογικὴ φωνή = 'theological term')
Preserves the structure of the argument closely
Correctly renders παρακαλοῦμέν as 'I implore' (1st person plural used as epistolary singular)
Maintains Basil's tone of humility and pastoral concern

issues: 'need of the questioner' — Greek ἐπιθυμίας = desire/longing, 'need' softens slightly
'It feels like a betrayal' — same issue as A: κίνδυνος προδοσίας = 'danger of betrayal'
'championing the truth' — Greek συνηγορίᾳ = advocacy/defense, 'championing' adds a slight connotation of active promotion vs. legal defense
'intellectual gifts' — Greek ὁρμαῖς = impulses/promptings, not 'gifts'; this is an interpretive shift
strengths: Much more natural modern English prose; paragraph breaks aid readability
Preserves the structure and argument faithfully
Correctly handles the epistolary plural
'harm the cause by my weakness' is a cleaner rendering than A's version
Tone is warm and direct — fits Basil's pastoral voice well

issues: Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added, not in source

'I sent them in good health' — Greek προεπέμψαμεν = 'we sent them on'; person is correct (epistolary plural) but 'in good health' modifies the sending, while Greek μεθʼ ὑγείας more closely means 'with/in health'
'so long as the Holy One ordains' — Greek οἰκονομῇ ὁ ἅγιος = 'the Holy One arranges/manages'; 'ordains' is slightly more authoritative than the Greek
'I to the end of my days' — awkward insertion mid-sentence
Prose reads somewhat stiffly in places
strengths: Faithful to the overall meaning and structure
'angel of peace to help and accompany them' accurately renders εἰρηνικὸν ἄγγελον βοηθὸν καὶ σύμπορον
Captures Basil's warm, affectionate tone
Final sentence is well-rendered

issues: 'Gods grace' missing apostrophe (minor typographical)
'angel of peace as their helper and companion on the road' — σύμπορον = fellow-traveler, 'companion on the road' is good but slightly expansive
'your present responsibilities' — Greek φροντίδων = cares/concerns, 'responsibilities' is a mild interpretive shift
'My hope is that' — restructures the Greek slightly from a purpose clause (ἵνα)
strengths: Excellent modern prose; paragraph breaks improve readability
Captures Basil's warm, personal tone very naturally
'I do not think you will find anyone who loves you more or values your friendship more highly' — superior rendering of the Greek
Final sentence is crisp and effective

issues: 'The famine is still with us' — Greek λιμός = famine, correctly rendered; however this is a strong opening that slightly restructures the Greek which begins 'Not yet has the famine released us'
'summon the holy brothers to your side' — Greek μεταστεῖλαι τοὺς εὐλαβεστάτους τῶν ἀδελφῶν = 'summon the most devout of the brothers'; 'holy' slightly changes εὐλαβεστάτους (most reverent/devout)
'friends further along the way' — adds 'along the way' not explicit in Greek τοῖς ἔμπροσθεν (those ahead/further on)
strengths: Clean, natural modern English throughout
Correctly captures the kinship detail (αἵματος γάρ ἐσμεν τοῦ αὐτοῦ = 'we share the same blood')
Paragraph breaks aid readability
Basil's practical, caring pastoral voice comes through strongly
Well-structured rendering of the medical situation

issues: 'The death is still with us' — CRITICAL ERROR: Greek λιμός = famine, NOT death (θάνατος). This is a significant mistranslation
'partly by the duty of distribution' — Greek οἰκονομίας = management/distribution of relief; 'distribution' alone loses the administrative connotation
'in mere conventional language' — slightly archaic phrasing
'Receive my entreaty that you will give him the usual protection' — restructures and slightly obscures the Greek which asks Eusebius to 'take up your customary role' (τὰ συνήθη προστῆναι)
Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added
strengths: Mostly preserves the structure of the letter
Correctly renders the kinship detail
Captures the overall pastoral concern
Final sentence about the letter of recommendation is clear

issues: 'I believe Your Excellency already knows' — restructures the Greek opening which says 'The presbyter of this place, I think, has long been known to your excellency, that he is my foster brother'
'I'm confident you'll do everything' — Greek πάσῃ δυνάμει προαιρῇ = 'you choose/prefer with all your power'; 'I'm confident' is interpretive addition
'someone I consider a second self' — Greek οὓς ἀντʼ ἐμαυτοῦ ἔχω = 'those whom I hold as myself'; rendered correctly in sense but shifts from plural to singular
'Simply that he not lose his current tax assessment' — Greek παλαιὰν ἀπογραφήν = 'old/former assessment', not 'current'; this reverses the meaning
'I'm particularly worried' — Greek γίνωσκε δέ μοι πάνυ ἐπιμελές = 'know that it is very important to me'; 'worried' is interpretive
strengths: Very natural, readable modern English
Good paragraph structure
Captures Basil's direct, practical tone well
Tax-related vocabulary is handled well overall

issues: Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added
'a foster brother of my own' — correct rendering of σύντροφος
'a second self' — good rendering of ἀντʼ ἐμαυτοῦ
'his old rating' — correctly renders παλαιὰν ἀπογραφήν (old/former assessment)
Prose is somewhat stiff in places ('I am specially anxious lest any injury should be done')
'Really he takes no little trouble' — slightly awkward phrasing
strengths: Very faithful to the Greek structure and order
Correctly renders παλαιὰν ἀπογραφήν as 'old rating' (vs. A's incorrect 'current')
Preserves Basil's appeal structure faithfully
'let me rather say, on your own' — captures the Greek μᾶλλον δὲ ὡς ἑαυτοῦ well

issues: Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added
'our men are so afraid of the winter' — Greek κατέπτηξαν τὸν χειμῶνα = 'cowered before the winter'; 'afraid' is acceptable but slightly less vivid

'can hardly bear even to put their heads outside their houses' — Greek μηδὲ τὸ μικρότατον προκύπτειν τῶν δωματίων = 'not even to peek out the tiniest bit from their rooms'; 'put their heads outside' is a reasonable paraphrase
'stale and uninteresting' — Greek ἕωλόν ἐστι καὶ ψυχρόν = 'stale and cold'; 'uninteresting' softens ψυχρόν which literally means cold/flat
Some sentences are quite long and convoluted
'said and did many painful things' — adequate but could be more precise
strengths: Comprehensive and faithful rendering of a long, complex letter
Captures the winter scene vividly
Handles the ecclesiastical politics accurately
Correct rendering of Demophilus and the Constantinople situation

issues: CRITICAL: This translation bears almost no relation to the source text. The Greek discusses winter snow in Cappadocia, news from Antioch, Demophilus in Constantinople, and a church schism. Translation B fabricates entirely different content about finding a trustworthy messenger, a bishop breaking communion, and appealing to Western bishops.
The entire second and third paragraphs are fabricated — nothing about Demophilus, Constantinople, or the schism appears
The bracketed editorial note about 'Eastern Nicene churches' is invented context not in the source
This appears to be a hallucinated translation that addresses a completely different letter
strengths: The English prose itself is well-written and natural
The opening about difficulty finding a messenger has a loose connection to the source's opening about finding a letter-carrier

issues: Omits the opening: Greek begins 'Having many reasons to thank the Lord for what we were deemed worthy of during our visit' — translation A drops the formal structure

'the greatest blessing was getting to meet you' — collapses Greek μέγιστον ἀγαθὸν ἐκρίναμεν τὴν γνῶσιν τῆς σῆς τιμιότητος = 'we judged the knowledge of your honour the greatest good'; loses the formal epistolary language
'who they are inside' — Greek ἐν τῇ διαθέσει τῆς ψυχῆς = 'in the disposition of the soul'; 'who they are inside' is overly casual
'Your actions already have' — somewhat loose rendering of τὴν ἐκ τῶν πραγμάτων ἔχοντες μαρτυρίαν = 'having the testimony from deeds'
Overall too informal for Basil's register; feels modernized beyond what the source warrants
strengths: Core meaning is preserved
Readable and accessible
The soldier/Christian distinction is captured

issues: Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added
'mercies vouchsafed to me in my journey' — somewhat archaic phrasing
'Play the man; be strong' — renders ἀνδρίζου τοίνυν, καὶ ἴσχυε faithfully but somewhat woodenly
'I have evidence in what you have done' — slightly compressed but accurate
strengths: Very faithful to the Greek structure and content
Preserves the formal epistolary register appropriately
'the perfection of love to God' correctly renders τῆς πρὸς Θεὸν ἀγάπης τὸ τέλειον
'not by the style of his dress, but by the disposition of his soul' — excellent rendering
Maintains Basil's authoritative yet warm pastoral tone

issues: Bracketed note '[the Praetorian Prefect...]' is editorial addition, not in source
'I couldn't stay silent' — interpretive addition; Greek says he 'dared' (ἀπετόλμησα) to give the letter
'a kind of peace offering' — Greek ἱκετηρίας = supplication/supplicant's branch; 'peace offering' shifts the metaphor
'I'm counting on your own sense of moderation to do the heavy lifting' — very colloquial rendering of τὸ μέτριον ἱκανὸν δυσωπῆσαι; 'heavy lifting' is anachronistic
'I'm nobody important' — overly casual for Greek ἡμεῖς οὐδενὸς λόγου ἄξιοι = 'we are worthy of no account'
strengths: Very readable modern English
Captures the power dynamics between Basil and the Prefect
Good paragraph structure
The overall argument and rhetorical strategy come through clearly

issues: Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added
'intrude upon your excellency' — μεγαλοφυΐᾳ = greatness of nature/magnanimity; 'excellency' is conventional but loses the specific Greek term
'propitiatory symbol' — ἱκετηρίας = supplication/supplicant's branch; 'propitiatory symbol' is closer but still not exact
'moderation itself may be able to conciliate' — somewhat convoluted phrasing
Final sentence is slightly awkward in structure
strengths: Very faithful to the Greek word order and structure
Preserves Basil's careful diplomatic language
'by the mere force of truth' — good rendering
Maintains the formal register appropriate for addressing a prefect

issues: Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added
'For I lent you a kindness, which I ought to get back with interest' — Greek ἐδάνεισα γάρ σοι χρέος ἀγάπης = 'I lent you a debt of love'; 'kindness' softens ἀγάπης (love)
'Pay me, then, my friend, by paying me a visit' — ὦ φίλη κεφαλή = 'O dear head' (a term of endearment); 'my friend' loses the warmth of the idiom
Prose is somewhat stiff
strengths: Closely follows the Greek structure
The financial metaphor (capital/interest) is preserved well
Captures the playful tone of the letter
Short letter rendered concisely

issues: 'You owe me one' — extremely colloquial for Greek Ἔχω σε χρεώστην ὀφλήματος ἀγαθοῦ = 'I hold you as debtor of a good debt'
'I did you a kindness' — same issue as A: ἀγάπης = love, not just kindness
'And this is the kind of interest even our Lord approves of' — Greek οὐ παραιτεῖται = 'does not refuse/reject'; 'approves of' is a stronger positive than the Greek double negative
Use of italics (*you*) is an editorial choice not warranted by the source
Very informal register throughout — perhaps too casual for Basil writing to a fellow bishop
strengths: Lively, engaging modern English
The playful tone is captured well
Financial metaphor (principal/interest) is clear
Readable and entertaining

issues: Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added
'uncertain rumours' — Greek πεπλανημένας τὰς φήμας = 'wandering/misleading reports'; 'uncertain' is slightly weaker
'your excellency' — Greek ἡ ἐπιστολὴν τῷ ποθεινοτάτῳ = 'most beloved'; later uses εὐλάβειαν = 'reverence'; mixing up which honorific goes where
Final clause is a single long sentence that could be clearer
strengths: Faithful to the structure and content
Place names (Samosata, Batnæ, Antioch) all correctly rendered
Count Saturninus and Sanctissimus correctly identified
Captures the warmth of Basil's friendship

issues: Bracketed geographical notes are editorial additions not in the source — adds significant non-source material
'I've been completely in the dark' — very colloquial for Greek ἠγνόησα = 'I was ignorant'
'conflicting reports' — Greek πεπλανημένας τὰς φήμας = 'wandering/misleading reports'; 'conflicting' is interpretive
'That's why I haven't written' — loses the Greek's more careful phrasing: 'wherefore I did not write frequently'
'Don't forget that' — added imperative not in the source; Greek simply says 'whom you chose from the beginning to love and hold among your most intimate'
Breaks the letter into more paragraphs than the source warrants
strengths: Very readable and natural modern English
The emotional warmth comes through clearly
Good use of paragraph breaks for readability
The final appeal is rendered with warmth and directness

issues: Bracketed note '[a senior priest]' is editorial addition

'You can probably guess from this letter how much yours meant to me' — significantly restructures and simplifies the Greek opening, which says 'How much you gladdened us with your letter you can probably guess from the very things you wrote'
'a stream always tells you something about its source' — Greek ὁλκὸς μὲν ὕδατος = 'a channel/conduit of water'; 'stream' is slightly different
'I have to admit something strange happened' — Greek ἄτοπόν τε καὶ πολὺ τοῦ εἰκότος παρηλλαγμένον = 'absurd and much divergent from what is likely'; paraphrased loosely
'You'd make me happier than a miser receiving a pile of money' — Greek φιλοπλούτοις = 'lovers of wealth'; 'miser' adds a pejorative not in the source
'no secretary available' — Greek γραφέων = scribes/writers; 'secretary' is a modernization
Overall significantly loosened from the source throughout
strengths: Excellent modern prose; very readable
Captures the emotional dynamic well
Good paragraph structure
The complaint about lack of scribes is clearly rendered

issues: Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added
'The pureness of heart' — Greek καθαρὸν τῆς καρδίας = purity of heart; 'pureness' is slightly unusual English
'your excellency' — Greek τελειότητός σου = 'your perfection'; using a conventional title instead of the specific Greek term
'I am thinner than ever' — attempts to capture the wordplay but slightly awkwardly
'misers' — Greek φιλοπλούτοις = 'lovers of wealth'; 'misers' adds pejorative connotation (same issue as A)
'caligraphist' — unusual English spelling (calligraphist is standard)
strengths: Very faithful to the Greek structure
Water/stream metaphor rendered accurately
The paradox of joy and frustration is well captured
Preserves the detail about scribes (καλλιγραφούντων/ταχυγράφων) with precision

issues: Person shift: ἡμᾶς (us) consistently rendered as 'me/I' throughout
ἱκετεύει (supplicates) softened to 'asked' -- loses the urgency of supplication
δι' ἡμῶν (through us) rendered as singular 'me'
κυνείαν (canine/dog-like) lost entirely; replaced with interpretive 'deceptively mild appearance'
Bracketed glosses add explanatory material not in the source
strengths: Highly readable modern English prose
Captures the Odyssey parallel effectively
Good paragraph structure aids comprehension
Final aphorism rendered cleanly

issues: Slightly stiff phrasing in places ('exceedingly probable')
'intoxication of hostility' is creative but slightly over-literal for ἐμπαροινήσαντος
Opening 'Up to this time I used to think Homer a fable' is awkward syntax
strengths: Preserves 'dog's fierceness and fury under a woman's form' -- accurate for κυνείαν
Maintains closer structural order to the Greek
Retains more of the original vocabulary precision
'supplicates you by my means' preserves both ἱκετεύει and δι' ἡμῶν

issues: Person shift: ἡμᾶς (us) rendered as 'me' in 'charging me'
παρετηρησάμην (I was careful/cautious) rendered as 'I have declined' -- loses the nuance of deliberate caution
'inventing alternatives to the ancient faith' slightly interprets παρὰ τὴν ἀρχαίαν περιεργάζεσθαι πίστιν
strengths: Clean, well-organized paragraph structure
Strong final paragraph on faith, baptism, and doxology
Captures Basil's practical pastoral tone well
'resist all innovation concerning them' is precise for καινοτομίαν διαφεύγειν

issues: Same person shift: ἡμᾶς rendered as singular
'plainly charging me' -- ἐναργῶς is 'clearly/plainly' so this is acceptable but slightly clunky
'in still plainer colors' adds a metaphor not in the Greek (φανερωτέρους = 'more manifest')
'modes of address' for προσηγοριῶν is slightly vague
strengths: Close structural fidelity to the Greek sentence order
Preserves the logical flow of the argument well
'shun all innovation about them' is good for καινοτομίαν διαφεύγειν
Accurate rendering of the baptism-doxology parallel

issues: ὄψον (relish/side dish) rendered as 'delicacy' -- overstates the word
Omits the address line 'To Antipater, the governor'
Slightly loose with 'good medicine' for πρὸς ὑγίειαν ἀρκεῖ (sufficient for health)
'the proverb' left unexplained in both but A adds a bracketed note
strengths: Captures the light, humorous tone of the letter perfectly
Clean prose with good rhythm
Final sentence builds to a pleasing climax
'nursemaid of men' effectively renders κουροτρόφον

issues: 'dainty' for ὄψον is imprecise -- ὄψον means relish or cooked food, not something dainty
'healthy fare' slightly paraphrases πρὸς ὑγίειαν ἀρκεῖ (sufficient for health)
'I shall think nothing like cabbage' is slightly awkward English
strengths: Includes proper address line
Close to the Greek structural order
Good rendering of the Homeric references
'nursing mother of men' effectively captures κουροτρόφον

issues: Person shift: ἡμᾶς (us) rendered as 'I' in 'must not I owe you'
'return you thanks in words' misses ἀπὸ γλώττης which implies in-person, face-to-face thanks
'the great pleasure of your society' is slightly archaic
Header 'ST. BASIL OF CAESAREA' added -- not in source text
strengths: Preserves the double-thanks structure clearly
'exceedingly gratifying on its own account' accurately renders διὰ τὴν οἰκείαν αὐτοῦ φύσιν
Maintains the warmth appropriate to Basil's friendly correspondence

issues: Same person shift: ἡμᾶς rendered as 'I'
'doubly indebted' slightly reinterprets διπλῆν ἔλαβες χάριν (received double grace/thanks)
'the friend who made it happen' somewhat interprets τὸν τῆς κατασκευῆς αὐτοῦ προεστῶτα (the one who oversaw its preparation)
strengths: 'thank you in person' better captures ἀπὸ γλώττης than A's 'in words'
More natural, flowing English prose
Good paragraph structure
Warm, personal tone fits Basil's friendly register

issues: Person shift: ἡμῖν (for us) and later plural forms rendered as singular 'I/me' throughout
'I am not able to flee' -- slightly awkward opening
'as to liberty of life the birds are almost as far ahead of me' is clunky syntax
'I am thinner than ever' simplifies αὐτὸς ἐγὼ ἐμαυτοῦ (I myself thinner than myself)
strengths: Accurate rendering of the quartan ague description
'cobweb' for ἀραχνίου is precise
Captures the medical detail well, appropriate for a letter to a physician
Good rendering of the final prayer

issues: Same person shift from plural to singular throughout
Bracketed gloss for quartan ague adds information not in source
'I was thin before' is a loose rendering of αὐτὸς ἐγὼ ἐμαυτοῦ (I myself thinner than myself)
strengths: Excellent readability with well-structured paragraphs
'every gust of wind is more dangerous to me than a great wave is to sailors at sea' flows naturally
Captures Basil's self-deprecating humor about his thinness
'remote corner of the world' vivid rendering of ἐσχατιάν
Warm, personal tone appropriate to writing a close friend

issues: Person shift: δοκοῦμεν (we seem) rendered as 'I seem'
παρακαλοῦντες (exhorting, participle) restructured into infinitive 'to exhort'
'I wish to tell you that I seem to be still alive' is somewhat flat
Slightly stilted overall phrasing
strengths: Accurate rendering of the geographical difficulty of communication
'punished for their faithfulness' good for δίκας διδόντες ὑπὲρ τῆς εὐσεβείας
Preserves the letter's two-part structure: practical news then spiritual request

issues: Same person shift: first-person plural rendered as singular
'I want you to know' somewhat interprets σημαίνοντες (signifying/indicating)
'scattered across the world' slightly expands ἐν τῇ διασπορᾷ (in the dispersion)
strengths: Clear, direct prose that reads naturally
Good paragraph break between practical and spiritual content
'Ask the Lord to ease my afflictions' is direct and effective
Captures the urgency and weariness of Basil's late correspondence

issues: 'great power of speech' slightly softens λαλίστατος (most talkative/chatty) -- removes the teasing quality
ἐοίκαμεν (we seem) rendered implicitly as first person singular
'think well of me' slightly softens μεμνημένος ἡμῶν τὰ εἰκότα (remembering us as is fitting)
strengths: Captures Basil's gentle teasing tone well
Clean, readable prose throughout
'those who first showed affection should be repaid in kind' is an effective closing
Good balance of warmth and mild reproach

issues: Same softening of λαλίστατος
'exhibiting yourself by your eloquence' is awkward for σεαυτὸν δεικνύναι διὰ τῶν λόγων
'paid in their own coin' introduces a monetary metaphor absent from the Greek (τοῖς ἴσοις = with equal things)
'I hope this description will continue to fit you' is somewhat stilted
strengths: Close structural order to the Greek
Preserves the contrast between being disliked for Basil vs disliking Basil
Accurate rendering of the Neocaesarea reference

issues: ἀνυποπτότερον (less suspicious) lost entirely -- rendered as 'better'
'crawling with enemies' adds vivid imagery not in the Greek (πεπληρωμένης = filled)
'who has never in his life stooped to servility' is interpretive for ἀλλότριον ἔχει θωπείας ἀνελευθέρου τὸ ἦθος
τὸν ποθεινότατον ἀδελφὸν Ἀκάκιον rendered as 'our very dear brother Acacius' but context unclear -- source says wanting not to cause delay to Acacius
Parenthetical '[probably Gregory of Nazianzus]' adds editorial content
strengths: Highly readable and engaging prose
Captures the political frustration effectively
Good paragraph structure separating the travel issue from the Gregory discussion
Final rhetorical question rendered with force

issues: Also loses ἀνυποπτότερον but slightly less egregiously ('thinking it better to write by means of strangers')
'what possible good could accrue to the cause' is somewhat stilted
'from a lower standpoint' for χαμόθεν (from the ground) is acceptable but slightly interpretive
strengths: 'full of enemies' is literal and accurate for πεπληρωμένης
'to whom anything like mean servility is unknown' closer to ἀλλότριον ἔχει θωπείας ἀνελευθέρου
Preserves more of the Greek sentence structure
More restrained and literal throughout

issues: 'Peradventure one of you may ask' is archaic English
'Shall not Christ's worshippers...be made famous and glorious' is somewhat overwrought
'Shall He not display the brightness of their radiant splendour as He does the beams of the sun' is convoluted
ποθεινότατοι softened from 'most longed-for' to 'my dear friends'
strengths: Accurate rendering of the lamp-on-lampstand metaphor
Preserves the athletic/contest imagery (ἀγών, σκάμμα, στέφανοι)
Full rendering of the theological content
Scriptural quotation accurately included

issues: εὐλαβείας (piety/reverence) rendered as 'devotion' -- less precise theological term
The rhetorical question sequence simplified and condensed from the Greek
σκάμμα (wrestling pit/arena) not distinctly rendered -- merged with 'arena'
καρτεροψυχίαν (steadfastness of soul) rendered as 'zeal' -- significant vocabulary loss
strengths: Clean, modern prose that reads beautifully
Good paragraph structure
'fill you with courage, strengthen your souls' is effective
Captures the encouraging, pastoral tone of Basil

issues: 'I had also hoped' slightly restructures the temporal sequence of the Greek
'I would make you a tender of my advice' is archaic and awkward
'a right conclusion of the matter calls for my intervention' slightly reorders the Greek
'do not be backward in coming to me' is stilted
strengths: Accurate rendering of the geographical references
'νέος φοιτῶν παρὰ γέροντι' accurately rendered as 'the young man should come to the old'
μεσιτείας correctly rendered as 'intervention'
Preserves Basil's tone of gentle authority

issues: μεσιτείας (mediation/intervention) rendered as 'involvement' -- less precise
'matters that call for my involvement if they are to reach a proper conclusion' slightly reinterprets the Greek
'do not hold back from coming' is slightly bland for μὴ κατοκνήσῃς
strengths: Clear, well-organized paragraph structure
Natural, flowing English prose
'the younger man should visit the older' reads more naturally than A's version
Good balance of directness and courtesy
Captures Basil's practical, no-nonsense tone

issues: Salutation altered: source has 'Augustinu Nebridius' (Nebridius writing TO Augustine), trans_a renders as 'To Augustine — Nebridius sends greetings' which is correct but adds words not in source
'manganis' (devices/contrivances) rendered as 'agency' — loses the connotation of magical tricks or stage machinery
'medicamentis' (drugs/potions) omitted entirely from the list of methods
Structural reorganization: Latin's long flowing period broken into shorter paragraphs and sentences, losing the breathless philosophical questioning style
'phantasticum/phantasia' terminology simplified to 'imagination' throughout — loses the technical philosophical register
strengths: Core argument about dreams and heavenly powers is faithfully conveyed
The body-soul examples (thirst/drinking, hunger/eating) are accurately rendered
Final self-deprecating remark about obscurity and inexperience preserved well
Very readable and clear English prose
Captures Nebridius's intellectual curiosity and warmth toward Augustine

issues: Prose is dense and heavily Latinate — long subordinate clauses mirror Latin but feel labored in English
'manganis' rendered as 'magic' which is one possible reading but adds a connotation not necessarily intended
'phantasticum' and 'phantasia' rendered as 'imaginative faculty' and 'imagination' — acceptable but inconsistent
Register feels more like a Victorian translation than a late antique personal letter — overly formal
Sentence beginning 'But if they actually do these things in their own body' runs very long and is hard to parse
strengths: Extremely close to the Latin in structure and content — nearly word-for-word
'medicamentis' (enchantments) is preserved unlike in translation A
The full argumentative chain is intact with no omissions
Salutation correctly rendered
Philosophical terminology handled with care

issues: Salutation simplified: source has 'Gaio Augustinus' — trans_a renders as 'Augustine to his friend Gaius' adding 'friend' not in source
'prudentissimae Caritati tuae' (your most wise Charity) rendered as 'your excellent judgment and generous spirit' — loses the formal epistolary 'Caritas' address convention
'non vulgariter candidum' (not commonly bright/pure) rendered as 'far beyond what is common among mortals' — 'mortals' is an addition
'a faece corporis' (from the dregs of the body) rendered as 'the body's clouding influence' — softens the visceral metaphor
Structural reordering: sentences broken into shorter units, paragraphed differently from source
strengths: All major content points preserved: memory of Gaius, his debate style, sending writings, epistemology of truth, human fallibility, encouragement
The light-of-truth passage is beautifully rendered
Captures Augustine's warm, intellectually generous tone well
The 'dew from the mists of human frailty' preserves the Latin metaphor nicely
Final passage about the veil of the body and God's providence is accurate

issues: Prose style is heavily Victorian — 'approved yourself', 'avails it', 'what is men's common lot'
'prudentissimae Caritati tuae' rendered as 'your eminent wisdom and charity' — preserves 'Caritas' but in an archaic way
Register feels stiff and formal rather than Augustine's characteristic warmth
'prope discussis corporis tegumentis' (almost as if the coverings of the body had been torn away) is well rendered but in stiff English
Some phrases feel like translation-ese rather than natural English
strengths: Extremely faithful to source text in both content and structure
No omissions or additions of substance
Latin sentence structure closely mirrored
Salutation accurately rendered with letter number and date
'a faece corporis' rendered as 'darkening influence of the body' — retains meaning if not exact metaphor

issues: Salutation: 'papae' rendered as 'Father' — acceptable but 'papa' in this period means bishop, not specifically 'Father' in the modern sense
'promptum reddens salutationis officium' (rendering the ready duty of greeting) rendered as 'a salutation due to you, and readily rendered by me' — slightly reordered
'germanissimum' (most closely connected/most genuine) rendered as 'very near and dear' — loses the kinship connotation
Jerome's characteristically sharp, efficient Latin style is somewhat smoothed out
'oppido te salutant' — 'oppido' (exceedingly) rendered as 'warmly' which softens the superlative
strengths: All content accurately conveyed: previous letter via Asterius, commendation of Praesidius, monastery hardships, John 16:33 reference, greetings to Alypius
Correctly identifies this as Jerome writing to Augustine
Two-section structure of the letter preserved
Scripture reference properly included
Closing formula accurately rendered

issues: COMPLETELY FABRICATED: This is not a translation of the source text at all
Source is Jerome's letter to Augustine (Ep. 39) commending the deacon Praesidius; translation_b is about 'Augustine to Antoninus' discussing pastoral burdens
Wrong sender (Augustine instead of Jerome), wrong recipient (Antoninus instead of Augustine)
No content from the source text appears anywhere: no mention of Asterius, Praesidius, the monastery, Alypius, or John 16:33
Entire letter is invented content about episcopal weariness and Matthew 11:30
strengths: The fabricated English prose is well-written and stylistically plausible as an Augustine letter
Register of the invented text sounds appropriately Augustinian (warm, pastoral)

issues: COMPLETELY FABRICATED: This is not a translation of the source text (Ep. 42)

Source is a short, pointed letter demanding Paulinus repay his epistolary debt after two summers of silence and requesting the book against pagans; translation_a invents an effusive first-contact letter about spiritual friendship

No content from source appears: no mention of brother Severus, two summers of thirst in Africa, the book against heathen worship, the year-long fasting from Paulinus's pen, or greetings to Romanus and Agilis
The entire letter is fabricated content about first meeting, renunciation of wealth, communion of saints
Appended 'Context' note contains information not relevant to this specific letter (Ep. 42 is a follow-up, not initial contact)
Adds 'Alypius sends greetings' which is not in the source
strengths: The fabricated prose is eloquent and stylistically convincing as Augustinian writing
Would fool a reader unfamiliar with the actual letter

issues: Some phrasing is archaic: 'art daily giving away what is your own' — 'art' feels dated
'flagitaremus' (we should have to demand) rendered as 'we should have to claim' — 'claim' is slightly weaker than the Latin's insistence
'tam opima mensa' (so rich a feast) is well rendered but the extended food/fasting metaphor feels slightly awkward in English
'parum nobiscum irascuntur, si parum diligunt' rendered correctly but somewhat stiffly
strengths: Faithfully renders all content: Severus as messenger, two summers in Africa, the book against heathen worship, the fasting/feast metaphor, greetings to Romanus and Agilis
Preserves Augustine's playful, teasing tone — the mock indignation at Paulinus's silence
Structure mirrors the source closely
The witty closing ('they would be less provoked...if they loved you less') is accurately captured
Salutation with full formal address preserved

issues: 'Immanitatis vestrae famosissimum scelus' — 'enormous crime' captures 'famosissimum scelus' but 'immanitatis vestrae' (of your savagery) is folded into description rather than placed emphatically first as in Latin
'iudiciorum rectorum calcatus est terror' — 'trampled in scorn the reverence due to equitable enactments' slightly expands the Latin
'collatis singulis nummis' (by contributing individual coins/pennies) rendered as 'raise a subscription in pennies' — good but 'subscription' is slightly anachronistic
strengths: All content faithfully preserved: the massacre of 60, blood in streets, Roman laws trampled, emperor's authority scorned, ironic offer to replace Hercules
The savage irony of offering red ochre to make Hercules blush is brilliantly captured
Augustine's furious, sarcastic register is perfectly maintained
The devastating closing demand — give back lives as we give back your god — hits with full force
Vocabulary is precise: 'rubricam' as 'red ochre', 'manganis' as 'artisans', 'sculpitur, tornatur et ornatur' fully rendered

issues: COMPLETELY FABRICATED: Not a translation of the source text at all
Source is Augustine's blistering denunciation of the colony of Suffectum for murdering 60 Christians over a Hercules statue; translation_b is about baptism of catechumens addressed to Boniface
Wrong recipient (Boniface instead of the magistrates of Suffectum), wrong subject matter entirely
No content from source appears: no massacre, no blood, no Hercules, no Roman laws, no ironic offer to replace a statue
Fabricated theological discussion about John 3:5 and the thief on the cross
strengths: The fabricated prose is theologically coherent and sounds like Augustine
Well-structured argument about baptism that is internally consistent

issues: Adds bracketed context note about Jerome and the Galatians dispute — not in source text, though helpful
'Sinceritatem tuam' (your Sincerity) rendered as 'the request I made when you were here' — drops the formal address term
'compresbytero' (fellow presbyter) — Jerome calls himself presbyter here; translation correctly renders this but adds no note about the significance

'motum eius, quem non frustra timui' (his agitation, which I did not fear without reason) rendered as 'the sharpness of his response — which, understandably, has left me genuinely uneasy' — 'genuinely uneasy' slightly softens 'non frustra timui' (not without reason feared)
strengths: Core content fully preserved: reminder about forwarding letter to Jerome, enclosure of correspondence copies, request for honest feedback
Augustine's careful diplomatic tone is well captured
The key point — 'send your criticism to me, not to him' — is accurately and clearly rendered
Natural, flowing English prose
Captures Augustine's anxiety about the Jerome dispute

issues: Prose is somewhat stiff and formal — 'mea intercessio extitit onerosa' rendered as 'been burdensome...as an intercessor requesting any favour' is accurate but awkward
'pro tua sancta prudentia' (according to your holy prudence) rendered as 'your pious wisdom' — accurate but stilted
Register feels more like a Victorian scholarly translation than a personal letter
Long subordinate clauses mirror Latin too closely for natural English reading
strengths: Extremely faithful to every detail of the source text
No omissions or additions of substance
'Sinceritatem tuam' preserved as 'sincere friend'
Latin structure closely followed
All diplomatic nuances preserved: the moderation vs. vehemence contrast, the request for correction

issues: COMPLETELY FABRICATED: Not a translation of the source text at all

Source is Augustine's fierce challenge to a Manichaean presbyter (successor of Fortunatus), demanding he answer theological objections or leave town; translation_a is about oaths sworn by martyrs' names
Wrong recipients (presbyters and people of Hippo instead of a single Manichaean teacher), wrong subject matter entirely
No content from source appears: no Manichaean dualism, no soul/body argument, no Fortunatus, no 'Beware of dogs' citation, no demand to leave
Fabricated pastoral letter about Matthew 5:34-37 and commercial oaths
strengths: The fabricated prose is well-crafted and could pass as Augustinian pastoral writing
The closing aphorism is memorable

issues: Opening editorial note ('A short and stern challenge to some Manichaean teacher...') is added context not in source — though this is standard in scholarly editions
'manganis' context: the 'magic' connotation from Manichaeanism is not directly addressed but implied
Some phrases run long in English: 'lest its insidious and pestilential power should do harm' is accurate but dense
strengths: Every argument faithfully rendered: evasion is futile, death vs. spiritual death, soul-body dualism refuted, God's fear of Darkness, dogs/canker citations, Fortunatus challenge, demand to leave
Augustine's combative, prosecutorial tone is perfectly captured
Scripture references (Philippians 3:2, 2 Timothy 2:17) correctly cited
The escalating rhetorical force of the Latin is preserved in English
The ultimatum at the end carries full weight

issues: 'parentum iure vincat affectum' (conquers the affection due to parents) rendered as 'has a stronger claim than love of kindred' — 'kindred' is broader than 'parentum' (parents specifically)
'Cui si ullus esset consulendi modus aut finis bonis' — the conditional is slightly simplified
'iuris publici rigore' (rigour of public law) rendered as 'rigour of the civil law' — acceptable but 'civil' is less precise than 'public'
Some Victorian phrasing: 'I have by this time well earned exemption' is slightly stiff
strengths: All content faithfully preserved: love of fatherland, Calama's transgression, plea for mercy, distinction between innocent and guilty, offer of financial restitution, deprecation of severe punishment
Nectarius's formal but earnest rhetorical style is well conveyed
The key argument — bishop's duty is welfare not punishment — accurately rendered
Salutation properly identifies Nectarius as sender and Augustine as recipient
Closing formula preserved

issues: Major fabrications: 'I am a pagan myself, as you know' — nowhere in the source text; Nectarius does not identify as pagan

Invented details: 'mob attacked the Christian church, assaulted clergy, and caused damage to church property' — the source is vague about what happened ('non levi populi sui erratu'), these specifics come from other letters in the correspondence, not this one
Source mentions love of fatherland exceeding love of parents — omitted entirely
Source mentions being born in Calama and rendering services — reduced to generic 'citizens of Calama'
Source's careful formal rhetoric (addressing Augustine's learning, the bishop's duty under divine law) replaced with blunt modern paraphrase
Appended 'Context' note contains interpretive claims not supported by this letter's text
The entire argumentative structure is replaced with a modern advocacy-style plea
strengths: Gets the basic situation right: plea for mercy after civil disturbance in Calama
Correct sender (Nectarius) and recipient (Augustine)
The core request — mercy over punishment — is preserved
English prose is clear and compelling

issues: Adds bracketed context note about Fortunatus — not in source but helpful
'Laus et praedicatio administrationis tuae et fama praeclara' — slightly compressed in rendering; 'praise and good reputation' covers it but loses the triple emphasis (praise, proclamation, fame)
'Eximietati tuae mea intercessio extitit onerosa' rendered with the negative framing preserved but slightly smoothed
'integrum' (upright/blameless) rendered as 'upright' — accurate
strengths: All content faithfully preserved: praise of Generosus's administration, first-time request, reference to letters to Fortunatus, confidence in Christian judgment
Tone is appropriately formal and deferential
The key contrast — never before burdened you, but now compelled — is clearly conveyed
Salutation accurately rendered
Natural, readable English

issues: Prose is somewhat stiff and formal — long subordinate clauses mirror Latin closely but read awkwardly in English
'although the praises and favourable report...' — the opening sentence is very long and hard to parse
Register feels more like a scholarly edition than a personal letter of commendation
'been burdensome to your Excellency as an intercessor requesting any favour' — accurate but clunky
strengths: Extremely faithful to source — near word-for-word accuracy
Triple emphasis preserved: 'praises and favourable report...illustrious good name'
No omissions or additions
All formal address terms preserved
Letter number and date included

issues: LARGELY FABRICATED: Invents most of the letter's content

Source is Augustine urging Volusianus to read Scripture (especially the Apostles, then the Prophets) and to write back with questions; translation_a invents a discussion about the incarnation and divine condescension
No content from source about reading Apostles first then Prophets — the specific recommendation structure is lost entirely
Source's key practical point about written correspondence being better than in-person discussion (because of scheduling and because unsuitable people intrude) is entirely omitted
The incarnation discussion ('how can the infinite God be contained in a human body') is fabricated — not in this letter
The mother's prayers ('sanctae matris tuae votis') are omitted
The description of Scripture as 'sincera et solida res' that does not rely on 'fucatis eloquiis' is omitted
strengths: Gets the basic setup right: Augustine writing to Volusianus encouraging engagement with Christianity
The fabricated prose sounds authentically Augustinian in register

issues: 'fucatis eloquiis' (painted/artificial eloquence) rendered as 'artificial eloquence' — accurate but loses the metaphor of cosmetics/painting
'linguae tectorio inane aliquid ac pendulum crepitat' rendered as 'giving forth with flattering voice a vain and uncertain sound' — good attempt at a difficult phrase but slightly smoothed
Some sentences are very long and hard to follow in English, especially the final sentence about written vs. oral communication
Register feels scholarly/Victorian rather than warmly pastoral
strengths: Every detail faithfully preserved: mother's prayers, description of Scripture's nature, recommendation to read Apostles then Prophets, invitation to write back, argument for written over oral communication
The practical reasoning about schedules and intrusive people is accurately conveyed
Closing metaphor about books being available at the reader's convenience is well rendered
Salutation with full formal address preserved
No fabrications or additions

issues: Date given as 413 when source says 410
'praeconia' rendered as 'eulogies' is acceptable but slightly elevated; 'praises' would be more direct
Slightly awkward phrasing: 'nevertheless I cannot but be grateful for the goodwill therein manifested towards one so insignificant'
'desideratissimo' as 'greatly longed for' is slightly stiff
strengths: Faithful clause-by-clause rendering of the Latin
Preserves the formal epistolary structure and salutation formula
Correctly renders 'exiguitatem meam' as 'one so insignificant'
Postscript 'Et alia manu' correctly handled

issues: 'Gratias ago plurimum quod me litteris tuis exhilarare dignatus es' — 'dignatus es' (you deigned/condescended) is lost, becoming casual 'thinking of me'
'certum facere de salute vestra' — 'vestra' (your, plural/formal) not reflected
'desideratissimo' rendered as 'I long to see' adds specificity not in the Latin
Dashes and em-dashes modernize the tone significantly, losing Augustine's formal register
'quae tuae Benignitatis epistola continet' — 'Benignitatis' as formal title dropped
strengths: Highly readable modern English
Core meaning preserved throughout
Postscript handled well with markdown formatting
Natural flow that a modern reader would find accessible

issues: Slightly verbose in places, typical of older translation style
'ancillis Dei' as 'nunnery' is an interpretive rendering rather than literal 'handmaids of God'
strengths: Accurately renders the sender as Evodius writing to Augustine, matching the source
All four questions faithfully reproduced
Theological vocabulary precise: 'rational soul', 'origin of souls', 'species'
Scripture quotation (1 Peter 3:18-19) accurately rendered
Complex subordinate clauses preserved from Latin structure

issues: WRONG LETTER ENTIRELY: source is Evodius to Augustine about theological questions; translation_b is about Marcellinus's arrest

Wrong recipient: Aurelius instead of Augustine
Wrong content: political arrest narrative vs. theological questions about Christ's soul and harrowing of hell
This is a fabricated translation that bears no relationship to the source text
strengths: The English prose itself is well-written, though it translates the wrong text entirely

issues: Adds a lengthy editorial preface summarizing the letter's contents not present in the source fragment
'occupationes villicas' (country/estate occupations) rendered as 'cares of wars and arms' — significant semantic shift

strengths: The theological content, while not matching the short source fragment, is coherent

issues: Source says 'occupationes villicas' (estate/rural occupations); translation says 'asked me to explain the Catholic Church's position' — complete fabrication of the framing

The content, while historically plausible for Augustine on Donatism, is not a translation of this source text
strengths: Excellent English prose quality

issues: Slightly archaic phrasing in places ('Macte virtute', 'esteemed your Blessedness with becoming reverence')

strengths: Correctly identifies sender as Jerome writing to Augustine, matching the source

'omnes haeretici detestantur' correctly rendered
Closing formula 'domine venerande, et beatissime papa' accurately translated

Bullet-point format completely alien to the epistolary source

Historically accurate about Pelagian condemnation, though not related to this source

issues: Some Victorian-era phrasing ('assuredly you would not have done unless')

strengths: Correctly identifies recipient as Largus and sender as Augustine

'Initium sine providentia, et finis cum poenitentia' beautifully rendered
Augustine's warm, pastoral voice clearly present
Rhetorical structure of the Latin preserved throughout

issues: WRONG LETTER ENTIRELY: source is to Largus about vanity of worldly honors; translation_b is to the people of Caesarea about a disgraced bishop

No element of the source text about worldly vanity, deceitful pleasures, or the contrast between worldly and pious hope is present

issues: Scholarly apparatus (footnotes) included in the translation body

Structural fidelity excellent — follows Greek clause order

issues: Bracketed explanatory glosses [a major Neoplatonist philosopher...] add content not in the source

'ψευδεπίγραφον' (falsely inscribed/titled) simplified to 'call myself your student' losing the nuance of claiming a title falsely
strengths: Very readable modern English
Core philosophical content preserved
Thyrsus-bearer explained in brackets for accessibility
Julian's enthusiastic, classicizing voice reasonably captured
Good flow and natural sentence construction

issues: Scholarly footnotes embedded in translation body affect readability

Julian's classicizing, literary style well captured

issues: 'ἐπιτρέψαντες οὖν σοι τὴν τῶν ὅπλων χρῆσιν' — 'entrusting you with a military command' goes beyond the Greek, which says 'entrusting you with the use of arms'
'τοῦ συντάγματι' (corps/regiment) rendered as 'household guard' with bracketed gloss — slight interpretive addition

strengths: Clean, readable modern English
Core meaning preserved throughout
Herodotus allusion correctly rendered
Explanatory brackets add useful context without disrupting flow

issues: 'Ὢ παρ' ἐλπίδα σεσωσμένος ἐγώ' — 'How little hope I had of surviving' changes the nuance slightly; source is more 'O I who have been saved beyond hope'

Bracketed historical gloss about Chalcedon tribunal is an addition
strengths: Julian's informal, personal voice well captured
Clear and natural English prose
Correctly preserves the complex political nuance about Constantius
The hydra metaphor and its clarification preserved

issues: Archaic phrasing: 'Suffer me to say', 'cast their baleful eyes', 'come hither'

All content preserved including the important qualifier about Constantius's nature
'ἐποφθαλμιῶντα' well rendered as 'cast their baleful eyes on all men'
The entire passage about the court, the invitation, and the emotional appeal all accurately rendered
Julian's classicizing voice with its literary allusions preserved

issues: Bracketed glosses ('[Christians]', '[Bishop of Alexandria...]') add substantial editorial content

Clean modern English that reads naturally

issues: Slightly archaic: 'One who had been banished', 'forthwith'

'There is a place for you here' — fabricated; not in the source

issues: Archaic in places: 'Come hither', 'task your strength'

issues: Adds bracketed gloss '[a true pagan Greek, devoted to the old gods and culture]' not in source -- an interpretive addition

issues: Slightly formal/stiff in places but faithful

issues: MAJOR: Translation is truncated -- cuts off mid-sentence after the Theocritus quote, missing roughly the last third of the letter

issues: 'The joy is doubled' -- source says 'there is a greater occasion for pleasure' (πλείων = 'more/greater'), not 'doubled'
'mutual intimate' simplifies the Greek 'through an intimate/familiar man' (δι' ἀνδρὸς οἰκείου)

Captures the warm, friendly tone
Core meaning preserved despite minor imprecisions

issues: Slightly awkward: 'It is an occasion to rejoice the more' -- grammatically clunky

strengths: Very close to Greek sentence structure
Accurate rendering of τροφέα as 'custodian'
Preserves the conditional logic of the final sentence faithfully
Includes scholarly context about the letter's authenticity

issues: 'A chatterbox' -- λάλος means 'talkative/chattering' so this is acceptable but slightly more colloquial than the source warrants
'natural philosophy would have me believe' -- source says ἡ φύσις ('nature') not 'natural philosophy'; slight over-interpretation

Final sentence slightly simplified compared to the Greek's more complex financial metaphor
strengths: Excellent naturalness -- reads like polished English prose
The sweetheart/lips simile is rendered vividly and accurately
Ball-throwing metaphor captured well

issues: Somewhat stilted English in places: 'I am abashed into admitting' is grammatically awkward

'I am eager to score off you in both ways' -- slightly misleading colloquial use of 'score off'

strengths: Extremely faithful to the Greek -- preserves nearly every nuance

Financial metaphor at the end preserved with precision

issues: MAJOR: Translation is truncated -- missing the entire second half about walking with Plato's Phaedrus, resting under plane trees and cypresses, and the final remark about sharing the experience
Adds a large bracketed editorial commentary paragraph not in the source at all -- fabricated interpretive content
'travel permit' for σύνθημα -- the word means 'token/signal/password'; 'travel permit' is an interpretation, not a translation
'strange dialects' for φωνῆς ἀλλοκότου -- more accurately 'strange/outlandish noise/voices'

issues: Slightly complex sentence structure that could be clearer

strengths: Complete translation -- includes the Phaedrus, plane trees, cypresses, and closing sentiment

issues: 'throw myself with all my strength' -- Latin says 'tota cum exultatione conplecti' = 'embrace with complete exultation/joy', not about physical strength

'every ounce of my mental energy is bent on this one thing' -- adds 'mental energy' where Latin has 'mens mea omni...cupiditate rapiatur' = 'my mind is seized by every desire toward that goal'; slightly different emphasis
'The sea all around me, and nothing but sea' set off as a separate verse line -- effective but adds 'nothing but sea' which is interpretive expansion of 'undique pontum'
'Quam, quam vellem' = 'How much I would wish' rendered as 'How I long' -- acceptable but loses the optative/subjunctive force
strengths: Excellent capture of Jerome's early voice -- passionate, self-lacerating, urgent
Prodigal son and lost sheep analogies rendered with real emotional force
Natural, vivid English throughout
Good handling of the devil/snares imagery
The closing image of the Holy Spirit as a gale is powerful

issues: Slightly more formal/Victorian in diction: 'Oh! That I could behold' feels dated

Less emotionally immediate than the source's raw passion
strengths: Very faithful to the Latin -- preserves structure and meaning precisely

Complete and accurate rendering throughout

issues: 'Melanium' -- should be 'Melania' (the well-known historical figure); this appears to be a variant/error in the name
'He has but now washed himself' -- slightly awkward phrasing

'Martinianus' vs source's 'Martianum' -- minor name variant
strengths: Very close to the Latin throughout

Biblical references properly cited

Complete translation including the closing greetings

issues: Slight reordering: 'I commenced to love you before I ever met you' -- Latin has 'ante incipio amare quam nosse' (love before know), 'met' is slightly different from 'know'
'the last penny' for 'novissimum quadrantem' -- acceptable modernization, though 'farthing' is the traditional rendering of quadrans
strengths: Natural, modern English that reads beautifully
Captures Jerome's characteristic self-deprecation vividly: 'just dust and worthless dirt -- even now, still alive, already ashes'

Good handling of the Rufinus/Melania reference

issues: 'Rhetitius' -- acceptable variant spelling

issues: Very minor: 'Autun' for 'Augustodunum' -- modern name, which is fine but loses the Latin flavor

issues: Title says 'Deacon of Antioch' but Latin says 'diaconum Aquileiae' = 'deacon of Aquileia' -- FACTUAL ERROR in the addressee's location
'collected' for 'mei paene memor fuerim' -- Latin says 'I was scarcely mindful of myself', not 'not as collected as usual'; this softens the meaning
'Hibera excetra' (Iberian viper) omitted in the main text -- replaced with 'some venomous critic' which is interpretive and loses the specific cultural reference
'someone once said' for the Horace quote -- Horace is not 'someone'; this is vague attribution
strengths: Excellent English prose -- vivid, energetic, natural
Captures Jerome's combative, self-dramatizing voice perfectly
The Horace verse rendered with punch

issues: Adds bracketed glosses: '[a 2nd-century BC Roman playwright]', '[an ancient pre-Roman people]' -- interpretive additions not in the Latin

'near-animals' -- interpretive addition; Latin says 'rudes...homines' and 'cruda rusticitas', which is about cultural roughness, not being sub-human
'shower of letters' -- Latin has 'litterario...officio' = 'literary/epistolary duty'; 'shower' is an intensification
'Laelius, in Cicero's dialogue' adds '[De Amicitia, On Friendship]' -- gloss not in source

Vivid, lively prose throughout
